one_line_takeaway: A human-validated audit of 1,080 GPT-4o/RCI code samples shows CodeQL and Semgrep agree with expert security labels on only ~61-65% of cases per-sample despite similar aggregate rates, arguing that single static analyzers are unreliable sole oracles and motivating a (conceptual) persistent-human-feedback RAG framework.
key_finding: Aggregate security rates hide large per-sample disagreement. Human ground truth: 61% of the 1,080 samples secure, 39% vulnerable. Semgrep called 60% secure and CodeQL called 80% secure, so Semgrep's aggregate rate looks close to human judgment -- yet per-sample only 65% of Semgrep's and 61% of CodeQL's verdicts matched the ground truth. Both tools frequently miss the target CWE while flagging non-target CWEs. This undermines using a single static analyzer as the sole security oracle and motivates persistent expert feedback. A secondary finding: GPT self-critiques that were specific/actionable yielded more secure code (64% secure) than general critiques (56%).
core_contribution: The paper's own empirical contribution is a human-validated audit of static analysis tools used as security oracles for LLM-generated code. The authors reuse GPT-4o code from Bruni et al. [7] (RCI prompting on LLMSecEval + SecurityEval, filtered to CWE Top-25 tasks), have trained human reviewers build a ground-truth of 1,080 samples labeled secure/vulnerable w.r.t. each sample's target CWE, and then measure how well CodeQL and Semgrep agree with that human ground truth on a per-sample basis. A secondary contribution is a survey of 2025 top-tier (A/A*) SE-venue papers characterizing benchmarks, languages, models, and evaluation practices (Tables I-V) -- this is the paper's survey, not the source of its empirical numbers. A third, explicitly conceptual (unevaluated) contribution is a proposed multi-agent framework that persistently stores expert feedback in a dynamic, dual-source RAG so LLMs can reuse it. The key methodological point is separating aggregate agreement from per-sample agreement: tools can match human security rates in aggregate while disagreeing heavily case by case.
threat_model: Not a classic attacker-victim threat model; the paper's security concern is the reliability of the evaluation pipeline itself -- treating single static analyzers (CodeQL/Semgrep) as the sole oracle for whether LLM-generated code is secure, which produces false-positive and false-negative security verdicts and misleading empirical conclusions. Within the proposed framework the authors do name one adversarial surface: the human-feedback ingestion path of the RAG is exposed to data poisoning and prompt-injection content, mitigated by a mandatory dual-review staging pool (new items get trust weight t(d)=0 until two independent reviewers approve). No attack is implemented or evaluated; the poisoning discussion is design rationale for the conceptual framework.
limitations: By the authors' own Threats to Validity: survey restricted to 2025 top-tier SE venues, excluding earlier work and dedicated security venues (USENIX Security, CCS, S&P) that may show different patterns. Ground truth rests on manual labeling, subject to human bias despite high kappa and expert oversight. Experiments cover only Python (LLMSecEval + SecurityEval), only one generator LLM (GPT-4o with RCI), and only two static analyzers (CodeQL, Semgrep), limiting generalization to other languages (especially low-level), domains, real-world systems, other LLMs, and other tools. Additional reviewer-noted scope limit: each sample was judged ONLY against its designated target CWE, so 'secure' samples may still contain non-target CWEs, and the validity of tools' non-target findings is unknown. The proposed framework is conceptual and not empirically evaluated -- no measurement of the persistent-feedback RAG's effect on security.

datasets_used: LLMSecEval (Python secure-code-generation prompts; source of tasks via Bruni et al. [7])
SecurityEval (Python secure-code-generation dataset; source of tasks via Bruni et al. [7])
Author-constructed human-validated ground truth of 1,080 GPT-4o+RCI code samples (108 CWE-Top-25 tasks x 10 prompts), labeled secure/vulnerable per target CWE
quantitative_results: Corpus size: 1,080 samples = 108 unique CWE-Top-25 tasks x 10 prompts each
Human ground truth: 61% of samples secure, 39% vulnerable
Semgrep classified 60% of samples as secure (aggregate)
CodeQL classified 80% of samples as secure (aggregate)
Per-sample agreement with ground truth: Semgrep 65%, CodeQL 61%
Table VI CodeQL: recall 0.34, precision 0.67, F-measure 0.45
Table VI Semgrep: recall 0.54, precision 0.52, F-measure 0.53
Table VI both-tools-in-agreement: recall 0.30, precision 0.76, F-measure 0.43
Specific GPT self-critiques -> 64% secure code vs 56% for general self-critiques
Motivation (cited from 2025 Stack Overflow survey, not the authors' result): 82% of developers used OpenAI GPT models in the past year
baselines_compared: CodeQL static analyzer (compared against human ground truth)
Semgrep static analyzer (compared against human ground truth)
Both tools in agreement / intersection (compared against human ground truth)
Human expert labeling used as the ground-truth reference, not a tool baseline
Generation configuration audited: GPT-4o with RCI (Recursive Critiques Improvement) prompting, from Bruni et al. [7]
